Sickle Cell Disease is caused by mutations in the HBB gene (Hemoglobin Subunit Beta). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Infancy onset, and is classified as High severity (Missense: Glu6Val (E6V)). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Base Editing, targeting Hematopoietic Stem Cells, currently at FDA Approved status with High evidence and an estimated 90% success rate. Base editing can precisely correct the HBB point mutation responsible for sickle cell disease. (Sources: ClinVar|GeneReviews|FDA)

Beta Thalassemia is caused by mutations in the HBB gene (Hemoglobin Subunit Beta). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Infancy onset, and is classified as High severity (Nonsense/Frameshift: Beta-globin deficiency). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Base Editing, targeting Hematopoietic Stem Cells, currently at Clinical Trials status with High evidence and an estimated 85% success rate. Base editing and HDR strategies target pathogenic HBB mutations. (Sources: ClinVar|GeneReviews)

Cystic Fibrosis is caused by mutations in the CFTR gene (CFTR Transmembrane Conductance Regulator). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Childhood onset, and is classified as High severity (Deletion: F508del). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Prime Editing, targeting Airway Stem Cells, currently at Preclinical status with High evidence and an estimated 78% success rate. Prime editing may restore CFTR sequence without double-strand breaks. (Sources: ClinVar|GeneReviews)

Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy is caused by mutations in the DMD gene (Dystrophin). It follows X-linked Recessive inheritance, typically has Childhood onset, and is classified as High severity (Deletion: Dystrophin loss). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Exon Skipping + CRISPR, targeting Muscle Stem Cells, currently at Clinical Trials status with High evidence and an estimated 80% success rate. Genome editing aims to restore the dystrophin reading frame. (Sources: GeneReviews|PubMed)

Huntington Disease is caused by mutations in the HTT gene (Huntingtin). It follows Autosomal Dominant inheritance, typically has Adult onset, and is classified as High severity (CAG Repeat Expansion: Toxic protein aggregation). The recommended CRISPR strategy is CRISPR Gene Silencing, targeting Neurons, currently at Preclinical status with Moderate evidence and an estimated 70% success rate. CRISPR approaches attempt to suppress mutant HTT expression. (Sources: GeneReviews|PubMed)

Hemophilia A is caused by mutations in the F8 gene (Coagulation Factor VIII). It follows X-linked Recessive inheritance, typically has Childhood onset, and is classified as High severity (Inversion/Deletion: Factor VIII deficiency). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Gene Addition + CRISPR, targeting Liver Cells, currently at Clinical Trials status with High evidence and an estimated 82% success rate. Editing strategies aim to restore functional Factor VIII production. (Sources: ClinVar|GeneReviews)

Marfan Syndrome is caused by mutations in the FBN1 gene (Fibrillin-1). It follows Autosomal Dominant inheritance, typically has Childhood onset, and is classified as Medium severity (Missense: Connective tissue defect). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Prime Editing, targeting Fibroblasts, currently at Experimental status with Moderate evidence and an estimated 68% success rate. Prime editing may correct pathogenic FBN1 variants. (Sources: GeneReviews)

Tay-Sachs Disease is caused by mutations in the HEXA gene (Hexosaminidase A). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Infancy onset, and is classified as High severity (Nonsense: HexA deficiency). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Gene Replacement, targeting Neurons, currently at Preclinical status with Moderate evidence and an estimated 72% success rate. Gene replacement is currently more feasible than direct editing. (Sources: ClinVar)

Phenylketonuria is caused by mutations in the PAH gene (Phenylalanine Hydroxylase). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Infancy onset, and is classified as Medium severity (Missense: Reduced PAH activity). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Base Editing, targeting Hepatocytes, currently at Preclinical status with Moderate evidence and an estimated 74% success rate. Base editing targets pathogenic PAH point mutations. (Sources: GeneReviews)

Spinal Muscular Atrophy is caused by mutations in the SMN1 gene (Survival Motor Neuron 1). It follows Autosomal Recessive inheritance, typically has Infancy onset, and is classified as High severity (Deletion: Loss of SMN protein). The recommended CRISPR strategy is Gene Replacement + CRISPR, targeting Motor Neurons, currently at FDA Approved status with High evidence and an estimated 88% success rate. SMN restoration therapies already demonstrate clinical benefit. (Sources: FDA|GeneReviews)
